title: Interference visibility as a witness of preparation contextuality via overlap inequalities
abstract: We show that standard multi-path interferometry, using only pairwise visibility measurements, provides an operational route to tests of preparation noncontextuality. Under ideal symmetric conditions, interference visibility directly encodes state overlaps, without requiring tomography or SWAP tests. For three paths, any jointly diagonalizable (coherence-free) description must satisfy ${V}_{12}^2+{V}_{23}^2-{V}_{13}^2\le 1$, where ${V}_{ij}$ are two-path visibilities. Pure qubit detector states violate this bound, achieving a maximal value of $5/4$. We generalize to arbitrary $n$-path interferometers and derive the tight qubit bound $S_n^{\max}=n\cos^2(π/2n)-1$ for all $n\ge3$, achieved by coplanar pure qubit states with uniform angular separation $π/n$. A robustness analysis yields explicit experimental thresholds. Under the operational equivalences used in overlap-based generalized noncontextuality frameworks, violations of these visibility inequalities also witness preparation contextuality. For $n$-cycle inequalities, only the pairwise visibilities appearing in the cycle need to be measured.
summary: This paper presents a way to detect preparation contextuality using simple interference visibility measurements in multi-path interferometers. It proves that for any n-path system, a specific inequality of visibilities can be violated by qubit states, and shows that larger systems actually make the test easier by lowering the required visibility threshold.
main_problem: Developing an operational method to test preparation noncontextuality and basis-independent coherence using standard multi-path interferometry without requiring full state tomography or SWAP tests.
main_result: The paper derives a tight qubit bound for n-path interferometers, $S_n^{max} = n \cos^2(\pi/2n) - 1$, and shows that violations of these visibility-based inequalities witness preparation contextuality.
method: The author uses Gram matrix positivity and trigonometric parametrization of Bloch vectors to derive overlap inequalities and optimizes the configuration of detector states to find the maximal quantum violation.
model_or_system: An n-path interferometer where a quantum particle is prepared in a pure state and coupled to detectors, with the detector states encoded as qubits (e.g., photon polarization).
key_observables: Two-path interference visibility (V_ij) and the n-cycle overlap expression (S_n).
important_parameters: The visibility threshold (eta_min) required to violate the classical bound, which scales as 1/sqrt(n-1) for large n.
main_assumptions: Ideal symmetric path amplitudes, pure qubit detector states, no higher-order interference, and uniform visibility reduction across all path pairs.
figures_summary: Figure 1 illustrates the optimal Bloch-vector configurations (equally spaced on a great circle) for n=3 and n=4 cases.
paper_structure: The paper introduces the problem of testing noncontextuality, defines the n-path interferometric model, derives the 3-path inequality, generalizes the result to n-paths, performs a robustness analysis for experimental visibility, and compares the method to existing techniques.
why_interesting: It provides a resource-efficient, scalable method for witnessing fundamental quantum properties like contextuality and coherence using only pairwise measurements, which is highly relevant to quantum optics and photonic quantum information.
analysis_basis: full PDF text, analyzed in chunks

figures.caption: FIG. 1. Optimal Bloch-vector configurations for the n-cycle overlap inequality. (a) n = 3: three coplanar detector states with uniform angular separation ∆= π/3 on the equatorial great circle of the Bloch sphere. (b) n = 4: four coplanar detector states with uniform angular separation ∆= π/4. In both panels the Bloch vectors lie on a common great circle and saturate the bound Smax n = n cos2(π/2n) −1, uniquely up to global rotations, reflections, and cyclic relabelings of the detectors.
